I'm going to tell you guys a story about this company called Vantage Hospitality. So my COO before she was my COO, calls me up because she knew that I was talented and she said, I need you to come help me consult with this company up in, I don't know, Delray Beach or something like that, Florida. And whenever we could drive up there, I would sit down and help these people and it was this guy here, a Harvard degree from the Harvard School of Business, and he would look me in the eyes and he'd expect me as a 21 year old at the time to consult his business on what they needed to do digitally in order to make revenue. So this guy had a massive micro-cap organization. So if you guys are familiar with the stock market, there's micro-caps, small caps, mid-caps, and large-cap corporations. And those caps are essentially the level and the amount of business that they do. So this is a micro-cap corporation publicly traded on the stock exchange that was looking to get merged or bought out by different organizations, but they needed to raise their revenue. So we came in, Kathy and I, which is my COO, and we set aside a goal to make sure that we raised this company's revenue at least 23% in under 120 days of us doing a consulting agreement with them. We had an $8,000 service fee for each of us, so $8,000 and an $8,000 herd, where each month they'd hand us an $8,000 check, but I would typically be able to cash and walk around this a little while. It felt pretty good. That being said, when I was getting those checks and I was doing the consulting, what we were there for was to create what I called a self-sustaining accountability model between three massive divisions within their overall organization that were communicating to each other. So I depicted this man's business, his name was Bernie, as a business that had three massive silos that were separate in their operations that were all working in tandem towards one goal that they weren't communicating about accomplishing between the three of them. So there'd be a division that would handle all the tech stuff, and there'd be a division that would handle all the traditional advertising. There'd be a division that would handle all the digital advertising. This company's in Utah, this company's in the headquarters in Delray, and this company's over in Cleveland, Ohio. So all these divisions aren't talking to each other, so they didn't even have a conference call, they had no email communication going through one another, and more importantly, they didn't even have some overall project manager overseeing everything these people were doing and creating the synergy between them. So the guy Bernie, he didn't want to hire anybody else because he didn't have the payroll dollars for it because keep in mind he was trying to get merged. So when you're trying to get merged, when you're in a microcap corporation or any corporation, I guess you don't want to spend more money. You look into economize and make more money up than things that you have. You look at it, the milk your assets would give common. So Bernie would come to me, he'd be like, Jeremy, I need you to find the delta, that's what this man would say. So he'd be like, I need you to find the one area that we're not seeing right now that WinFound will be like an engineer logic. What is the least input you can put in to get the most out? What is the one solution that just dominates all of the problems that we have into having solvable situations? What is the delta, Jeremy? So Kathy would do a lot of the groundwork and became coordinating any kind of meeting or event. She would also come and help out with different trainings in house. My job was literally to coordinate this self-sustaining accountability model somehow that I was going to create that I identified as the delta. I came into Bernie and I said, the reason that your people aren't working well together is because they have no synergy, they have no communication, and they have no overall accountability. Nobody is holding them accountable to get things done. I'm going to create a system for you, Bernie, that makes it so each one of these massive divisions of 70 plus people in each company hold each other's companies accountable by creating an overall perspective that the goals that they're accomplishing have joint team efforts. So I'm going to make each little line item thing they need to do. I'm going to make them all aware of it, first of all, and I'm going to say that this thing getting done affects this thing getting done, but this company is doing this one, and so this company is doing this one. So if this one doesn't get done, this company can't do this one. And so therefore this company needs to make sure that this company is doing step number one. Otherwise, they can't accomplish step number two. But I said the company who's hiring you guys, which is Vantage, is going to hold you guys accountable just to getting things done. I said therefore if you don't hold each other accountable to getting the individual steps done in this sheet, even though you know the other organizations that you don't communicate to need to do it, it'll affect your organization that needs to do the steps following it. Because you can't start those steps until the steps prior are complete. Now what happens after I say that to each one of these organizations over the course of about 30 days, and I consistently am vamping up this system, creating calls and a bunch of synergy between their organizations and creating communication lines and all that fun stuff? What happens? Great answer. They absolutely all started communicating with one another. They all created that master list of what needed to be done to accomplish a new set of goals that they all became aware of collectively. And more importantly, they all held each other accountable within their own organizations and the other's organizations in order to accomplish those goals and those tests on that individual sheet. Because I created a self-sustaining accountability model and a micro-cap organization that was on the Inc. 500 list that after 120 days had over 33% lived in their revenue, 10% above where a massive corporation was projecting it to be, with professional analysts who had Harvard degrees telling them how much more revenue they could make after a 21-year-old came in, consulted their business and created a self-sustaining accountability model with his current COO. It's pretty cool. All that being said, I come to you with counsel to let you know that the people within your digital agency that is not a micro-cap corporation with over 70 people in three different companies servicing their micro-cap corporation should also be able to successfully create a self-sustaining accountability model. How do you do it? Well, that master list of goals that I just mentioned, that your people all need to be aware they're attempting to accomplish, either for your clients or for your organization, they need to be aware that their ability to produce a result individually is contingent on their team members' abilities to produce and complete the steps necessary to get them to the point where they can take their action items. Meaning, I don't let people in my organization work if it doesn't have a flow or a process because the people who are next in the flow or the process are the people who are telling the person who's currently getting it done to get it done well so they don't all have to redo their work and to just get it done so they can accomplish their work by the deadline that I've set because they are all accountable to hit the deadline. They're all accountable for the result that the work produces and they're all accountable for making sure that one person gets it done in order for everybody else to get it done. Self-sustaining accountability model. I rarely have to come into my business and tell people to hurry or to hit deadlines anymore all because once again I have a self-sustaining accountability model. Now how do you keep them motivated? What do you do when everybody has a little bit of a large workload on their plate and have a little bit of issues waking up in the morning and being excited to go into work? First of all, this is where a company culture comes into play. If you guys need a company culture expert to follow, I recommend Jay Durand. Jay Durand is a friend and mentor out of Philadelphia who's going to be coming down here to Miami in August. I sat with Jay as a potential client and I talked to him for over four hours at a lunch meeting skipping three meetings that I was supposed to go to during that time where I was unfortunately just kind of pushing off with the things I needed to do because I was so interested and intrigued with the level of knowledge that Jay had. You see Jay reads six hours a day and he's a specialist in company culture and he came in and he shook me on what I was and wasn't doing inside of my business to motivate my people and to hold them accountable and create an overall culture that incites, excites and invigorates these people to keep moving. So Jay starts talking to me about all these tips and tricks. I'm going to give you guys some of the things that I use inside of my business and some of the things I've learned from experience through time to keep my people motivated. New abilities, goals and things that they can get and obtain are always things that make people more motivated. So the light at the end of the tunnel or the golden coins at the end of the rainbow there, you always need to keep it in sight for them. So you always first of all need to acknowledge progress. Progress requires acknowledgement and perfection requires forgiveness. David Meltzer, you have to be able to acknowledge the progress of your people as they're making it and as they're doing it. You see each person inside of your team has a different personality dynamic or how they need to be acknowledged. It might be on a one to one level, it might be in a team environment, it might be that they need to be held accountable by other team members and by you and they need to feel and see things around them all the time from messages and corponties of who you are and what you do. See Jay told me that you need to have an acronym in your business and I built one inside of mine, it's called Mark. It's Master Articulation Creativity and Results. So what I did is I swapped out the mark for the C and I made it like that. So it's Mastery Articulation Results and Creativity Mark. So inside of my business we believe in the mark system which is you need to be a master who has the ability to articulate themselves, who's also creative, who is result driven and result oriented. If you keep these four core values apart of you, you will be successful as a megalodon. You see your team members will respect you as a megalodon. You have these four characteristics. Your clients will get results if you hold these four characteristics. Every single thing in your personal life can be better if you hold these four characteristics. See your company needs characteristics or values that you can articulate, have on your wall, have signs in your emails or something. Just have reminders of values and core ethics and the fact that you don't deal with unmotivated people. You constantly have to have energy in your sleep. You'll see in every video that I speak I can just get hype and passionate in the moment, grab energy, create energy, manifest it, drive it into who I am and how I present myself. That's because at all times you have to be the best version of yourself. For what you want to see in your team you need to have in yourself. You see if you're not motivated and you're not somebody who's over the top all the time and you're not the ideal individual that you want to see in your team, you're not going to have that in your team. Lead by example. Keep them motivated by having team calls every day. Keep them motivated by constantly showing them results. Keep them motivated by telling them to stay motivated but give them personal leverage to. Talk about exciting team trips that you're going to take if they hit a certain reward level. Talk about individually what they are doing for success. Talk about as a team what you're doing for success. Talk about individually what they are doing for success. Shout them out in videos. Shout them out on the team calls. Do something that just acknowledges results and the abilities that your team has. You're going to keep them motivated by doing that. Focus them on the results that they're generating. Remember the substance-stating accountability model and keeping them motivated aren't that tough. You just got to know the tips and tricks that you just learned. This is phase number seven in the team building phase. I'll see you in the next video.
